old_claim.note: Deep Drilling Technology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from broad geothermal power to deep drilling; the node is anchored to early dry-steam geothermal electricity at Larderello in 1904.

source_shape.source_locator: Dry Steam Power Plant section describing dry-steam plants as the oldest geothermal power plants, first used in Italy in 1904.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: DOE describes early geothermal electricity as a dry-steam plant using hydrothermal fluids that are already mostly steam, with the oldest plants first used in Italy in 1904.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This rejects deep drilling as a prerequisite for the broad date anchor because natural dry steam could drive turbines before modern deep-drilling systems; drilling belongs on later enhanced or expanded geothermal extraction nodes.

ontology_before: The graph treated deep drilling as an enabling prerequisite for all geothermal power, which forced a later 1945 chronology onto a technology first demonstrated in 1904.
ontology_after: Broad geothermal power is anchored to early dry-steam electricity generation; deep drilling is left for later geothermal extraction, scaling, or enhanced-geothermal-system nodes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-10-energy-chronology-scope keeps geothermal power dated to 1904 and the direct deep-drilling edge absent.
would_reject_if: The geothermal node were narrowed to enhanced geothermal systems or modern drilled geothermal reservoirs.
A source showed that the first scoped geothermal-power demonstration required deep-drilling technology.
A later graph split introduced a separate early dry-steam geothermal node and moved deep drilling onto a narrower modern geothermal node.
short_reason: Early geothermal electricity used naturally occurring dry steam, so modern deep drilling is not a prerequisite for the broad geothermal-power node.
